coredata.dc:title: Aluminum current collector for high voltage Li-ion battery. Part II: Benefit of the En’ Safe® primed current collector technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Bizot, Chloé
Blin, Marie-Anne
Guichard, Pierre
Soudan, Patrick
Gaubicher, Joël
Poizot, Philippe
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the benefits of a carbon coated aluminum current collector specifically designed to promote LiNi1.5M1.5O4 (LNMO) as high potential positive electrode material. This underlayer allows efficient protection of the aluminum surface in 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] electrolyte for more than 100 cycles (C/2–D/2) in experimental conditions mimicking the electrochemical response of LNMO. The design of such coating appears therefore as a very encouraging strategy to make the use of imide-based electrolytes possible despite their corrosive properties towards aluminum. The as-developed carbon coating also reduces the interfacial impedance of an LNMO-based electrode by a factor of 5 in 1 M LiPF6/[EC/DMC] electrolyte therefore paving the way to accelerate the industrial deployment of safe “5V” Li-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coated current collector
5 V Li-ion batteries
LNMO electrode design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impact of the En’ Safe® coating on the Al current collector resistance to oxidation, short time tests
Impact of the En’Safe® coating on the current collector resistance to oxidation, long time tests
Impact of the En’Safe® coating on the current collector interfacial resistance

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms (first six cycles) obtained at 0.5mV.s−1 using 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] using a) uncoated Al and b) coated Al as working electrode.

a) Potentiostatic electrochemical test (current density (red) applied potential (blue)) of the En’Safe® current collector in 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC] replicating the galvanostatic charge/discharge profile of Fd
3 -
m-LNMO at C/5–D/5 rate (black dots). b) Cumulative anodic charge during potentiostatic cycling of the En’Safe® current collector (red curve) and double layer coated current collector (blue curve) in 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC] and overall anodic charge after CV test (6cycles between 2.8 and 5 V at 0.5 mV.s−1, green dot) in 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC]. Digital and SEM imaging of c) the pristine Al foil surface used to produce the En’ Safe® current collector, and d) Al surface of the En’Safe® current collector upon potentiostatic test and after coating removal. (The reader is referred to the web version of this article to better apprehend color references.)

Comparison of a)capacity retention b)relative discharge capacity and c)polarization cell evolution of LNMO half-cell upon cycling with uncoated Al foil current collector and coated current collector over 100cycles at C/2–D/2 rate between 3.5 and 5 V in 1 M LiPF6/[EC/DMC] at 22 °C. The reported data correspond to the average of three coin-cells and colored areas correspond to the standard deviation to take notice of the dispersion. d) Nyquist diagrams before and after cycling of the cell. EIS data were recorded at 22 °C.

head.ce:title: Aluminum current collector for high voltage Li-ion battery. Part II: Benefit of the En’ Safe® primed current collector technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université de Nantes, CNRS, Institut des Matériaux Jean Rouxel, IMN, F-44000 Nantes, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Université de Nantes, CNRS, Institut des Matériaux Jean Rouxel, IMN, F-44000 Nantes, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ARMOR SAS, 7 rue de la Pélissière, 44118 La Chevrolière, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ARMOR SAS, 7 rue de la Pélissière, 44118 La Chevrolière, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the benefits of a carbon coated aluminum current collector specifically designed to promote LiNi1.5M1.5O4 (LNMO) as high potential positive electrode material. This underlayer allows efficient protection of the aluminum surface in 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] electrolyte for more than 100 cycles (C/2–D/2) in experimental conditions mimicking the electrochemical response of LNMO. The design of such coating appears therefore as a very encouraging strategy to make the use of imide-based electrolytes possible despite their corrosive properties towards aluminum. The as-developed carbon coating also reduces the interfacial impedance of an LNMO-based electrode by a factor of 5 in 1 M LiPF6/[EC/DMC] electrolyte therefore paving the way to accelerate the industrial deployment of safe “5V” Li-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-growing demand for electrochemical storage devices notably to power various electric vehicles has led to an intense development of the LIBs market [1–3]. Today, industrial stakeholders give the priority to both energy and power densities as well as safety, cost and resource constraints aspects [4]. In this context, following the development of Ni-rich layered oxide-based batteries (LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2 (NMC 5/3/2), LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC 8/1/1), and LiNi0.9Mn0.05Co0.05O2 (NMC 9/0.5/0.5)), the deployment of high potential active materials [5] such as the spinel LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 (LNMO) [6], stands out in most industrial roadmaps just before the upcoming all-solid-state battery systems [7]. Indeed, its high operating potential (~ 4.7 V vs. Li+/Li) combined with a theoretical capacity of 148mAh.g−1 allows, in principle, to design high energy density and cobalt-free LIBs. Nevertheless, these highly sought batteries still remain at the lab scale due to significant degradation of cell performance, the rapid drop in cyclability being often ascribed to the electrolyte degradation at the positive electrode side [8–11]. As already underlined in Part I of the work, current commercial liquid electrolytes are usually formulated with organic carbonates and LiPF6 as the supporting salt. Indeed, the sensitivity towards moisture of this fluorinated salt enables efficient passivation of Al positive Current Collector (CC) [12–15]. However, this high reactivity combined to poor thermal stability [16] rises safety concerns. Furthermore, the limited suitability of this electrolyte for 5 V applications owing to the irreversible decomposition of solvents and salt at high potential − producing the so-called Cathode-Electrolyte Interphase (CEI) and promoting the dissolution of transitional metal [17] − is pointed as a serious bottleneck to the development of these “5V” systems [5,18]. On the contrary, imide-based lithium salts such as lithium bis(trifluoromethylsulphonyl)imide (LiTFSI) or lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (LiFSI) show higher chemical and electrochemical stability and are thus expected to stabilize the CEI [19,20]. However, the significant solubility of the aluminum-imide complexes [21] in organic carbonate solvents prevents the passivation of Al CC, which undergo anodic dissolution above ~ 3.7 V vs. Li+/Li [15]. Passivating agents can be added to imide-based electrolyte to circumvent this issue [15,22] but by playing with the dangerous HF chemistry. Other approaches revolve around the increase of the supporting salt concentration [23] or the substitution of common organic carbonate solvents [24] including the use of fluorinated solvents [25,26]. But for the latter, only NMC-related electrode materials were electrochemically assessed.

In this contribution, an alternative approach that consists in designing advanced and industry relevant coatings of the Al CC is proposed. Since 2015, ARMOR SAS has been designing and producing coated current collectors for lithium-ion batteries (LFP [27], NMC, NCA…), alternatives chemistries (MnO2, LiS), and super-capacitors. The En’ Safe® technology uses a thin conductive coating (~ 1µm-thick) made of a mixture of carbon and polymer anchored to a metallic substrate (Al or Cu CC). The presence of this thin carbon-based layer not only results in lower contact resistance but also improve the adhesion of the electrode leading to improved power performance and cycle life. Herein, the benefits of a coating for 5 V applications are disclosed. We demonstrate the latter can fully protect Al CC in corrosive electrolyte such as 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC], while it decreases the interfacial resistance with a LNMO-based electrode in passivating electrolyte.

Both 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] and 1 M LiPF6/[EC/DMC] electrolytes (EC:DMC = 1:1, v/v) were supplied by Solvionic (water content: <20 ppm) and used as received. The two tested current collectors were a 1000 series aluminum grade at 99.35 % with a thickness of 20 µm (referred to as uncoated Al) and an En’ Safe® current collector (provided by ARMOR SAS and referred to as coated Al). The coated current collector is made with the 1000 series aluminum grade coated with thin conductive layer composed of polymer and carbon additive (1.5 µm-thick). N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP, Alfa Aesar) LiNi0,5Mn1,5O4 active material (Targray), carbon black (C65, Timcal), and PVdF (Solef 5130, Solvay) were used as received.

Prior to testing, CC were dried at 90 °C under vacuum for 1 h. The ability of carbon coating to protect Al from anodic dissolution in 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] was investigated in an argon-filled glove box (Jacomex) at 27 ± 0.5 °C by cyclic voltammetry and potentiostatic methods using a VMP electrochemical workstation (BioLogic SAS, Seyssinet-Pariset, France) connected to a three-electrodes cell detailed in our previous study (Part I). Li metal was used as both reference and counter electrode, and all potentials in the text are reported vs. Li+/Li.

The positive composite electrode was fabricated with 93 % wt LNMO active material 2 % wt C65 and 5 % wt PVdF. The slurry was prepared by dispersing PVdF in NMP during 2 h at 60 °C with magnetic stirring. The C65 powder was added and dispersed in PVdF solution for 30 min with a stirring machine (IKA). Finally, the LNMO powder was added and the electrode slurry was mixed with the stirring machine during 75 min at room temperature. The total dry content was set at 50 % wt. The slurry was coated on CC then electrodes were dried from 65 °C to 130 °C in air during 1 h 15 and then maintained 1 h at 130 °C under vacuum. The loading of active material was 6 mg.cm−2 (0.7mAh.cm−2). The electrodes were calendared to reach a porosity of 35 %.

Half-cell tests were performed in SS 316 L CR2032 coin cells (S4R) using Li metal (Aldrich, Ø 15 mm) as negative electrode. A fiber glass separator (Whatman) was used and the volume of 1 M LiPF6/[EC/DMC] electrolyte was set to 200 µL. The positive electrode was punched as Ø1.27 cm2 disc. Prior to the assembly in glove box, the latter was dried at 90 °C during 1 h under vacuum.

The electrochemical performance of LNMO-electrode was also assessed with a VMP electrochemical workstation at both 22 and 50 °C by using the following cycling protocol. First, six charge/discharge cycles recorded between 3.5 and 5.0 V vs. Li+/Li were performed at C/5–D/5 rate. Then, the electrode was charged to 75% State Of Charge (SOC) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were then recorded in the frequency range of 994 kHz to 10 mHz, with a 5 mV sinusoidal amplitude after 1 h OCV period. Then, a galvanostatic cycling step at C/2–D/2 rate was performed for 100cycles. For comparison, EIS spectra was also recorded after cycling. Note that high-rate discharge performances were also evaluated before and after the cycling period (i.e., 100cycles C/2–D/2). The corresponding electrochemical procedure was based on two successive steps. First, the LNMO-electrode was fully delithiated (charged) at C/5 rate to 5 V followed by a constant voltage period at 5 V. The charge process stops when the charge current drops below a cut-off level equivalent to C/20 (i.e., constant current constant voltage (CC-CV) charge profile). Second, the electrode was lithiated to 3.5 V vs. Li+/Li successively at 20D, 10D, 5D, 2D, 1D, 0.5D, and 0.2D (i.e., j = 14.0, 7.0, 3.5, 1.4, 0.7, 0.35, and 0.14 mA.cm−2, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the En’ Safe® coating on the Al current collector resistance to oxidation, short time tests
ce:section.ce:para:
Following our previous benchmark work (Part I), Fig. 1
a shows the first six cyclic voltammograms obtained in 1 M LiTFSI/[EC/DMC] on uncoated Al current collector. As previously detailed in our statistical study, the current increase during the reverse scan and all along the following cycles is typical of an anodic dissolution process.

In order to assess the ability of our En’ Safe® coating to protect Al, the same electrochemical test was performed onto coated current collector. As shown in Fig. 1 b, the corresponding CV curves do not show the characteristic increase in current density above 3.7 V. Instead, the current increases in a multi-step fashion above 4 V. From our understanding, this electrochemical activity can be ascribed either to the coating oxidative degradation or more likely to that of the electrolyte at the carbon surface. Note that the decrease in current density upon cycling suggests that the system tends to stabilize. Supported by our statistical analysis over 30 samples of uncoated Al (Part I), the present En’Safe® coating undoubtedly allows a huge improvement for Al protection in these testing conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the En’Safe® coating on the current collector resistance to oxidation, long time tests
ce:section.ce:para:
To further support the capability of the En’Safe® coating to protect Al foil from anodic dissolution during the use of 5 V materials in 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC], CC were tested in conditions that mimic the galvanostatic cycling of Fd
3 -
m-LNMO phase in order to get the proper stability of the coating. Basically, the galvanostatic charge/discharge profile of Fd
3 -
m-LNMO (black dots in Fig. 2
a) was replicated by applying successive potentiostatic steps (blue line in Fig. 2 a). The duration of which were adjusted to a C/5–D/5 rate [28] for the six first cycles and C/2–D/2 rate for the next 100cycles. As shown by the red curve in Fig. 2 b the cumulative anodic capacity reaches 71mC.cm−2 after the first six cycles and finally 220mC.cm−2 upon 100cycles. Based on the results of the statistical analysis reported in our previous benchmark study (Part I), if the overall anodic charge exclusively stems from the anodic dissolution of Al (worst case conditions), the 220mC.cm−2 value would be associated with the occurrence of 15 pinholes of 0.13 mm diameter. These values advantageously compare to those obtained for the uncoated Al CC that shows an average of 4450mC.cm−2 (green point in Fig. 2 b) and 540 pinholes obtained after only 6CV cycles between 2.8 and 5 Vvs.Li+/Li at 0.5 mV.s−1. We note this voltammetry conditions correspond to as low as 8.7 h above the aluminum oxidation potential (3.7 V) compared to 450 h for the potentiostatic test. Furthermore, the cumulative anodic charge in Fig. 2 b presents a flattening behavior over time, which does not fit with a pitting mechanism of Al that would lead to an ever increasing current density (Fig. 1 a). After electrochemical test, the sample was collected and the carbon coating was removed from Al foil and characterized by digital microscopy and SEM.

The current collector aspect upon test appears very similar to that of the pristine Al surface from digital microscopy (Fig. 2 c-d) confirming no pinhole was formed. A closer look to the sample from SEM shows the vertical streak striations, typical of roll process, and perpendicular stripes that are due to the sample scrubbing (Fig. 2 c bottom). However, any damages of the Al surface that could resemble an oxidative attack could not be found despite several surface areas were examined. Accordingly, the cumulated charge density measured during potentiostatic cycling is most likely due to electrolyte oxidative decomposition at the carbon surface of the En’Safe® coating. To confirm the barrier property of the coating, an alternative current collector was designed by successively casting two layers of the same carbon-polymer materials. Despite the thickness of the overall double layer coating was indeed found to be doubled, the cumulative anodic charge measured in the exact same conditions (blue curve in Fig. 2b) exactly overlays that of the single layer sample. Therefore, if the anodic charge does indeed come from electrolyte degradation at the carbon surface, it appears the En’Safe® coating is dense enough to act as a barrier to the electrolyte and is able to protect Al CC up to 5 V in highly corrosive electrolyte such as 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the En’Safe® coating on the current collector interfacial resistance
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of the En’Safe® coating on the current collector interfacial resistance with a LNMO based electrode, galvanostatic tests were performed in coin cell using a standard (and passivating) electrolyte: 1 MLiPF6/[EC/DMC]. Note the polarization of the cell was described by calculating the average polarization cell (ΔV) for each cycle by subtracting the average discharge potential from the average charge potential [29] (see SI for details).

Fig. 3 compares the cycling behavior of LNMO electrode over 100cycles at C/2–D/2 rate with uncoated and coated Al foil as the current collector. Although the initial specific discharge capacity (Fig. 3 a) is similar (~ 135mAh.g−1), the capacity retention (Fig. 3 b) is improved thanks to the En’Safe® coating from 92 % of the initial capacity upon 100cycles to 94 % for uncoated and coated Al CC respectively. Interestingly, larger uncertainty of the electrochemical response (specific capacity and polarization, the two of them being most likely correlated, Fig. 3 b-c) is noticed for the uncoated CC (Fig. 3 b). We believe the use of a carbon coating leads to a better reproducibility because it limits parasitic reactions between the reactive aluminum surface and the electrolyte. Fig. 3 c presents the average polarization cell over the 100cycles performed at C/2–D/2 rate. As for the discharge capacity, the cell polarization calculated for the first cycle is similar for both coated and uncoated Al CC (~ 100 mV). However, the polarization increases faster in the case of uncoated Al foil over the first 25cycles and reaches 172 mV (against 128 mV for coated Al foil). Upon the last cycling, the polarization cell has increased by a factor of 2.7 for uncoated Al CC (272 mV) compared to only 1.7 for the coated Al, which clearly highlights the beneficial role of the En’Safe® coating in improving the current collector/electrode interfacial resistance. To further support these observations, EIS spectra were recorded at 75% SOC before and after cycling. In all cases (before/after cycling and with/without coating) the Nyquist diagrams exhibit two semi-circle in the 994kHz–1 Hz frequency range just before the Warburg diffusion domain as shown in Fig. 3 d. Before cycling, the total semi-circle domain impedance is clearly reduced in the case of coated Al (~ 10 Ω.cm2) compared to uncoated Al (~ 25 Ω.cm2). The benefit is all the more visible after cycling as the total semi-circle impedance reaches 100 Ω.cm2 in the case of uncoated Al foil whereas it remains 20 Ω.cm2 with the coated CC. Interestingly, the origin of the increase in total semi-circle impedance differs greatly between the two current collectors. In the case of uncoated CC, the first high frequency semi-circle shows a strong increase (~20 Ω.cm2 before and 84 Ω.cm2 after cycling) as well as the second semi-circle (10 Ω.cm2 before and 65 Ω.cm2 after cycling). For the coated CC, the impedance increase is stronger for the second semi-circle (4 Ω.cm2 before and 20 Ω.cm2 after cycling) whereas the high frequencies semi-circle hardly evolves and remains ~ 5 Ω.cm2. According to a more exhaustive study carried out by Pritzl et al. [30–32], the second mid-frequencies semi-circle could be attributed to the charge transfer resistance, related to the electrode/electrolyte interface and the first high frequencies semi-circle to electrode/current collector interface resistance. Our observations are in good agreement with this ascription as the use of a carbon coating greatly decreases the high frequency impedance. The decrease in interfacial resistance is expected to improve high-rate performance following a decrease of cell polarization. Indeed, the small specific capacity difference that is observed when cycling at C/2–D/2 (Fig. 3 b) is greatly enhanced at 20D ( Figure S1 ), since the discharge capacity of LNMO-electrode using coated CC is about 60mAh.g−1 whereas it drops to zero in the case of uncoated Al. To complement these observations, the same tests were performed at 50 °C. In short, the cyclability of LNMO-electrode drops rapidly in the case of uncoated CC due to pronounced increase in cell polarization ( Figure S2 ) which increases from 575 to 1080 mV in the case of uncoated Al and only from 100 to 220 mV for En’Safe® current collector. In contrast, the discharge capacity of the LNMO-electrode using coated CC remains in the vicinity of 120mAh.g−1 after 100cycles (against 20mAh.g−1 for uncoated Al). In the case of uncoated Al, the semi-circle strongly increases at 50 °C and only one semi-circle is observed on EIS spectra before and after cycling ( Figure S3 ). For the coated CC, two semi-circle can still be observed. Just like at 22 °C, the impedance of the second semi-circle strongly increases after cycling from 8 to 33 Ω.cm2 while the impedance of the first semi-circle that is initially higher compared to experiments at 22 °C, increases from 43 to 75 Ω.cm2 after cycling. This significant gain in terms of cell impedance at 50 °C thanks the En’Safe® coating is echoed in the discharge rate tests ( Figure S4 ) where the discharge capacity of LNMO-electrode using uncoated Al drops down to zero at 2D after cycling while it remains as high as 109mAh.g−1 with coated Al.

Following our previous benchmark study (Part I), we show here that the innovative En’Safe® coating, developed for “5 V” applications undoubtedly protects aluminum CC for as much as 100cycles in 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC] using potential and time conditions replicating real LNMO active material operation. Furthermore, the flattening cumulative anodic charge on cycling lead us to believe that such protection in most likely ensured for much longer cycling. Moreover, we studied the impact of our coating on interfacial resistance between the current collector and a LNMO-based electrode in a standard 1 MLiPF6/[EC/DMC] electrolyte. It appears that the use of this innovative En’Safe® coating can also greatly reduce such resistance and therefore limit the cell polarization increase at 22 °C and 50 °C. The qualification of this innovative En’Safe® coating assembled with a LNMO-based electrode in 1 MLiTFSI/[EC/DMC] is in progress. The presently developed En’ Safe® technology opens up therefore great promise to sustain the deployment of safer “5 V” Li-ion batteries.

Chloé Bizot: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Marie-Anne Blin: Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Pierre Guichard: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition. Patrick Soudan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Joël Gaubicher: Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Philippe Poizot: Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Nicolas Stéphant (IMN) for technical support regarding the SEM investigation. This work was funded by the National Agency for Research and Technology (ANRT) through an Industrial Research Convention (CIFRE) between IMN/CNRS UMR 6502 and ARMOR SAS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.elecom.2021.107008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
